articles.title: I Am a Terrible Sleeper, but This Clever Nighttime Trick Helped Me Fall Asleep in Minutes
articles.content:
我的大脑时间感极差。  
下午三点，当我需要回邮件、完成编辑、表现得像个正常成年人时，我的大脑却只想钻到桌子底下，和我的猫一起打个盹。但当晚上11点半，灯都熄了，我终于躺下时，它却突然想把生活中的所有未解决的问题都拿出来复盘一遍。  

我是不是在会议上反应怪异？我有没有关烤箱？我为什么说“听起来不错！”其实什么都不“不错”？我应该多拉伸吗？现在跑步还来得及吗？  

这根本不是在思考，而是我的大脑一下子打开了37个标签页，却从不告诉我哪个在播放声音。  

显然，我不是一个人。根据2024年美国疾病控制与预防中心的数据，30.5%的美国成年人在24小时内睡眠不足七小时，15.4%的人经常难以入睡。2025年国家睡眠基金会的一项调查也显示，68%的成年人至少有一些入睡困难。  

因此，当我听说有一种简单的心理小技巧可以快速让思绪安静下来时，我有点兴趣。不是完全相信，只是感兴趣。我试过太多睡前建议，知道“放松一下”不是个有效的助眠策略。  

但这个方法听起来有些不同，值得一试。在几个不眠之夜，它确实做了些我没想到的事：它给我的过度思考找了个出口。  

《读者文摘》的“读起来”新闻简报，为你提供整周的睡眠技巧、幽默、清洁、旅行、科技和有趣事实。  

安静入睡有时特别困难，因为当一切终于安静下来，大脑才有空间变得喧闹。  

据专门研究行为睡眠医学的阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦医学院临床神经学和精神病学副教授雪莉·哈里斯博士（Shelby Harris, PsyD）所说，当白天的干扰消失，大脑有了处理和担忧的空间，思绪就开始奔腾。这会让大脑和身体处于高度警觉状态。她说：“你的大脑是清醒的，身体是紧绷的。大脑的威胁检测系统往往持续开启，陷入反复思考和忙碌的思维中，试图解决问题或保持警觉。”  

所以，尽管身体可能已准备好入睡，但大脑仍像在完成一个班次。我的大脑通常从小任务开始，然后迅速升级为人生审计、五年计划和一些我并不想重访的记忆。  

这个技巧叫“认知洗牌”（cognitive shuffling），由认知科学家、西蒙弗雷泽大学的兼职教授吕克·P·贝杜瓦博士（Luc P. Beaudoin, PhD）创造，用于在睡前平息那些忙碌的思绪。贝杜瓦在1989年开始探索这个概念，当时他偶尔的失眠让他开始思考，是否可以引导大脑进入睡眠，而不是等它自己发生。这最终促使他开发了“认知洗牌”，这是一类技术，旨在让睡前的思绪更利于入睡。  

基本思路是：在你试图入睡时，给大脑提供一连串随机、容易想象的图像。贝杜瓦后来将这一技巧开发成mySleepButton应用程序，通过精心策划的提示引导用户进行洗牌。但也有DIY版本：从一个至少五个字母的简单、无聊的词开始，比如“cloud”。然后取这个词的第一个字母，想一些以该字母开头的词，每个词都简单想象一下，再继续下一个字母。  

所以，对于“cloud”，你可以从：仙人掌、杯子、卷心菜、牛仔。  

然后是：灯、柠檬、湖、梯子。以此类推。  

这些词不应该有联系。你不是在给自己讲睡前故事。你不是在想象一个牛仔扛着卷心菜在湖边爬梯子。混乱（和失眠）就在那里。关键是保持图像的随机性，让大脑无法将它们编成情节、计划或恐慌螺旋。  

哈里斯博士的版本遵循相同的一般原则，但方法不同：从一个类别开始，然后按字母顺序，想出每个字母的词，一边想象一边进行。  

例如，如果你饿了：A，苹果。B，香蕉。C，哈密瓜。  

你可以使用食物、动物、物品或任何容易想到的词。重点不是要令人印象深刻。没人会因为想到木琴而获奖。你只是想让你的大脑有事可做，而不是让它有有趣的事可抓。  

自然地，我立刻找到了一种复杂化的方法。我第一次尝试用“dance”这个词，看起来无害，直到我的大脑立刻补充：引爆器、损坏、黑暗。这根本不是我想要的温馨小幻灯片。  

我觉得我做得不对，但我决心继续尝试。  

认知洗牌有效，是因为它给你的大脑足够的事可做。  

试图强迫大脑空白可能适得其反。哈里斯博士称其为“最无效的策略之一”，因为不思考的努力往往会产生更多想法。不幸的是，这正是我告诉自己安静时大脑的反应。  

数羊对一些人有帮助，如果能吸引他们的注意力，哈里斯说，但认知洗牌可能更有优势，因为它既无聊又吸引人。“它足够无聊，但也足够吸引人，能平息其他部分的思维冒出来，”她解释道。  

在我尝试认知洗牌的第四晚，我换成了哈里斯的方法。限制自己在动物类别中感觉更安全，而不是我的大脑在“dance”上做的事。  

哈里斯说，认知洗牌可能帮助那些晚上思维活跃的人，尤其是如果他们在用它而不是强迫睡眠或空白大脑。如果你的问题像我一样，是反刍，它可能给你的大脑一个中性的任务可返回。  

南佛罗里达大学护理学院研究副院长、麦克雷睡眠研究实验室主任克里斯蒂娜·S·麦克雷博士（Christina S. McCrae, PhD）说，如果你半夜醒来，20分钟内无法再次入睡，因为你的思维又打开了，它也可能有用。  

但这并不适用于每个人。有些人晚上大脑并不特别忙碌，哈里斯说，即使过度思考的人也可能达到极限。“如果你发现你已经遍历了字母表，但无法安静下来，你可能需要尝试别的，”她说。事实上，白天练习正念可能有助于加强夜间技能。  

麦克雷补充了另一个重要提醒：“认知洗牌不是基于证据的技术，但它似乎与认知疗法有共同元素。”所以，当你的大脑不平静时，它可能在偶尔有用，但它不是护理的替代品。美国睡眠医学学会推荐认知行为疗法（CBT-I）作为成人慢性失眠症的治疗，麦克雷建议对慢性睡眠问题进行全面评估。  

这种帮助工具和实际治疗之间的区别与我的经验一致。长期来看，我的睡眠问题可能太大，单靠认知洗牌无法解决；我每晚并不总是成功。有时我卡在Q或X上，又开始翻来覆去。有时，我的大脑用中性词替换了失败、收件箱、预算。那时，我不再洗牌。我只是按字母顺序担心。如果连续几晚都这样，可能意味着你需要的不只是睡前小技巧。  

认知洗牌可能以几种可预测的方式出错：你可以把它搞得太复杂，选择带有情感包袱的词，或把游走的思绪当作失败。哈里斯说，挫折是简化的信号。如果一个类别变得太窄或你卡在某个字母上，放宽规则，选择任何容易的词。  

别把它放宽到问题解决的领域。我会避免任何与工作、金钱、健康担忧、家庭压力、关系冲突、政治或重大生活决策有关的词。如果一个词让你想，实际上，我应该做点什么，它可能不属于你的洗牌。  

具体词可能比抽象词更好。和平听起来很安静，但它可能导致一些严肃的宏观思考。枕头是好的。蜡烛不错。香蕉很棒。  

如果你的思绪在洗牌时游走，那不意味着整个事情都毁了。哈里斯说，要注意练习，当它漂移时把注意力带回来。  

对我来说，认知洗牌在重新引导我的思绪时效果最好，而不是作为睡眠本身的解决方案。这与麦克雷的警告一致：它可能值得一试，但不是基于证据的治疗。在更好的夜晚，认知洗牌软化了我大脑通常开始冲刺的那部分。一旦我停止过度思考，选择了温和、简单的提示，它给了我一些低风险的东西慢慢漂浮。在更难的夜晚，它更像是暂停而不是解决方案。如果我真的对某事感到焦虑，洗牌可以缓解边缘，但并不总是完全安静。我把它当作一个工具，而不是整个睡眠计划，我仍在与心理健康专业人士讨论其他可能有帮助的方法。  

所以，虽然认知洗牌不是完美睡眠的快车道，但它是大脑不停叙述的夜晚的一个小心理绕道。有时我的大脑跟着它走。有时它直接冲过去。但当它有效时，我与噪音有了一点距离。当你的大脑嗡嗡作响时，这可能就是全部的区别。  

关于专家  
在《读者文摘》，我们致力于通过有专业知识和经验的作家与相关、合格专家合作，制作高质量内容。我们依赖政府和专业组织、学术机构等可信的一手资料，以及我们作家的个人经验。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可信的来源支持，并随时间更新它们以确保准确和最新。阅读更多关于我们的团队、贡献者和编辑政策。  

这篇文章最初出现在《读者文摘》上。
articles.summary:
My brain has terrible timing.

At 3 p.m., when I need to answer emails, finish an edit and behave like a functioning adult, it would happily crawl under my desk and cuddle up with my cat for a nap. But at 11:30 p.m., when the lights are off and I am finally horizontal, suddenly it wants to workshop every unresolved issue in my life.

Did I respond weirdly in that meeting? Did I turn off the oven? Why did I say “Sounds good!” when nothing sounded good? Should I be stretching more? Is it too late to become a person who runs?

This is not thinking. This is my brain opening 37 tabs and then refusing to tell me which one is playing audio.

And apparently, I have plenty of company. According to 2024 data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 30.5% of U.S. adults got less than seven hours of sleep in a 24-hour period, and 15.4% had trouble falling asleep most days or every day. A 2025 poll from the National Sleep Foundation also found that 68% of adults have at least some difficulty falling asleep.

So when I heard about a simple mental trick that supposedly helps quiet your thoughts quickly, I was interested. Not convinced. Interested. I have tried enough bedtime advice to know that “just relax” is not a sleep strategy.

Still, this sounded different enough to test. And on a few restless nights, it did something I did not expect: It gave my overthinking brain a place to go.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more sleep tricks, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Why is it so hard to fall asleep sometimes?

Peacefully dozing off can sometimes feel especially difficult because when everything finally gets quiet, your brain has room to get loud.

According to Shelby Harris, PsyD, a clinical associate professor of neurology and psychiatry at Albert Einstein College of Medicine who specializes in behavioral sleep medicine, racing thoughts can take over once the distractions of the day fall away and your brain has space to process and worry. That can keep your brain and body in a state of hyperarousal. “Your brain is turned on; your body is tense,” she explains. “The brain’s threat-detection system often stays on, as it is stuck in ruminative and busy thinking, trying to solve problems or be alert.”

So though your body may be ready for sleep, your brain is still acting like it has a shift to finish. Mine tends to open with small tasks, then escalates quickly to life audits, five-year plans and memories I did not consent to revisiting.

What is the nighttime trick that helps you fall asleep quickly?

The trick is called cognitive shuffling, and it was created by Luc P. Beaudoin, PhD, a cognitive scientist and adjunct professor at Simon Fraser University, as a way to quiet those busy thoughts before sleep. Beaudoin began exploring the concept in 1989, when his occasional sleeplessness got him thinking about whether he could steer the brain toward sleep instead of waiting for it to happen on its own. That eventually led him to develop the cognitive shuffle, a category of techniques meant to make your bedtime thoughts more sleep-friendly.

The basic idea is to give your brain a stream of random, easy-to-picture images to focus on as you’re trying to fall asleep. Beaudoin later turned the technique into mySleepButton, an app that guides users through the shuffle with curated prompts. But there’s also a do-it-yourself version: Start with a simple, boring word that has at least five letters—something like cloud. Then, take the first letter of that word, and think of random words that begin with it, picturing each one briefly before moving on to the following letters.

So, for cloud, you could start with: cactus, cup, cabbage, cowboy.

Then move to: lamp, lemon, lake, ladder. And so on.

These words shouldn’t connect. You are not telling yourself a bedtime story. You are not imagining a cowboy carrying a cabbage up a ladder beside a lake. Chaos (and sleeplessness) lies that way. The whole point is to keep the images random enough that your brain can’t turn them into a plot, a plan or a panic spiral.

Harris’s suggested version follows the same general principle but takes a different approach: Start with a category, then run through the alphabet, thinking of a word within that category for every letter, picturing each one as you go.

For example, if you’re feeling hungry: A, apple. B, banana. C, cantaloupe.

You can use foods, animals, objects or anything that comes easily. The point is not to be impressive. Nobody is handing out awards for making it to xylophone. You’re just trying to keep your brain occupied without giving it anything interesting to latch on to.

Naturally, I found a way to complicate it immediately. My first attempt used the word dance, which seemed harmless enough until my brain immediately supplied: detonator, damage, darkness. Not exactly the cozy little mental slideshow I was going for.

I don’t think I was doing it right, but I was determined to keep trying.

Why does this work?

Cognitive shuffling works because it gives your brain just enough to do.

Trying to force your mind to go blank can backfire. Harris calls it “one of the least effective strategies out there,” because the effort of not thinking often makes more thoughts pop up. Which, unfortunately, is exactly how my brain behaves the second I tell it to be quiet.

Counting sheep can help some people if it holds their attention, Harris says, but cognitive shuffling may have an advantage because it is both dull and engaging. “It is boring enough but also engaging enough that it quiets the other parts of the mind from popping up and intruding,” she explains.

By the fourth night that I tried cognitive shuffling, I switched to Harris’s approach. Limiting myself to a category like animals felt safer than whatever my brain was doing with dance. Aardvark. Beaver. Cat. Dog. Earwig. OK, that last one made me squirm. But eventually, the list started to blur, and I couldn’t tell you which letter lost me to unconsciousness.

Does this always work?

No—and having realistic expectations for cognitive shuffling is important.

Harris says cognitive shuffling may help people who have very active minds at night, especially if they are using it instead of trying to force sleep or a blank brain. If your issue, like mine, is rumination, it may give your mind a neutral task to return to.

Christina S. McCrae, PhD, the assistant dean of research at the University of South Florida College of Nursing and the director of the McCrae Sleep Research Lab, says it may also be useful if you wake up in the middle of the night and cannot fall back asleep within 15 to 20 minutes because your thoughts have switched back on.

But it is not going to work for everyone. Some people just don’t have especially busy brains at night, Harris says, and even overthinkers may hit limits. “If you find that you’ve gone through the alphabet and it isn’t working to quiet your mind, you probably need something else to try,” she says. In fact, practicing daytime mindfulness may help strengthen that skill for nighttime.

McCrae adds another important caveat: “Cognitive shuffling is not an evidence-based technique, but it appears to share elements with cognitive therapy.” So it may be useful on the occasional night when your brain won’t settle down, but it is not a substitute for care if you’re having nightly trouble falling asleep. The American Academy of Sleep Medicine recommends cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia, or CBT-I, as a treatment for chronic insomnia disorder in adults, and McCrae suggests a complete sleep evaluation for chronic sleep problems.

That difference between a helpful tool and an actual treatment tracked with my own experience. Over the long term, my sleep issues might be too big for cognitive shuffling to solve on its own; I certainly didn’t have success with it every night. Sometimes I got stuck on Q or X and started tossing and turning all over again. Other times, my brain swapped neutral words for failure, inbox, budget. At that point, I was no longer shuffling. I was just worrying alphabetically. And if that keeps happening night after night, it may be a sign that you need more than a bedtime trick.

What mistakes will torpedo this method?

Cognitive shuffling can go sideways in a few very predictable ways: You can make it too complicated, choose words with emotional baggage or treat wandering thoughts like failure. Harris says frustration is a sign to simplify. If a category gets too narrow or you get stuck on a letter, loosen the rules and choose any word that comes easily.

Just don’t loosen them so much that you wander into problem-solving territory. I’d avoid anything tied to work, money, health worries, family stress, relationship conflict, politics or big life decisions. If a word makes you think, Actually, I should do something about that, it probably does not belong in your shuffle.

Concrete words can also be better than abstract ones. Peace sounds sleepy, but it can lead to some serious big-picture thinking. Pillow is good. Candle is nice. Banana is wonderful.

And if your thoughts wander as you shuffle, that does not mean the whole thing is ruined. Harris says to pay attention to the exercise and bring your mind back when it drifts.

So how well does this work—really?

For me, cognitive shuffling worked best when I treated it as a way to redirect my thoughts, not as a fix for sleep itself. That lines up with McCrae’s caution: It may be worth trying, but it’s not the same thing as an evidence-based treatment.

On the better nights, cognitive shuffling softened the part of bedtime where my brain usually starts sprinting. Once I stopped overthinking it and chose gentle, easy prompts, it gave me something low-stakes to slowly drift off to.

On the harder nights, it worked more like a pause than a solution. If I was genuinely anxious about something, the shuffle could take the edge off, but it did not always fully quiet things down. I’m treating it as one tool, not my entire sleep plan, and I’m still talking with a mental-health professional about what else might help.

So while cognitive shuffling is not an express lane to perfect sleep, it is a tiny mental detour for the nights when your brain will not stop narrating. Sometimes mine followed it. Sometimes it blew right past. But when it worked, I got a little distance from the noise. And when your mind is whirring, that can make all the difference.

RELATED:

  • A Viral TikTok Hack Promises Better Sleep During Flights. Here’s Why You Absolutely Shouldn’t Try It—And What to Do Instead
  • Stressed Out? This 3-Minute Task Can Boost Your Mood and Help You Focus
  • Doing This Simple Thing for Just 2 Hours a Week Can Actually Keep Your Brain Sharp

About the experts

  • Shelby Harris, PsyD, DBSM, is a board-certified behavioral sleep medicine specialist, a clinical associate professor of neurology and psychiatry at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and the author of The Women’s Guide to Overcoming Insomnia: Get a Good Night’s Sleep Without Relying on Medication.
  • Christina S. McCrae, PhD, DBSM, is a board-certified behavioral sleep medicine specialist, a professor and assistant dean of research at the University of South Florida College of Nursing and the director of the McCrae Sleep Research Lab.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Shelby Harris, PsyD, DBSM, behavioral sleep medicine specialist, clinical associate professor of neurology and psychiatry at Albert Einstein College of Medicine and author of The Women’s Guide to Overcoming Insomnia; interviewed, May 2026
  • Christina S. McCrae, PhD, DBSM, behavioral sleep medicine specialist, professor and assistant dean of research at the University of South Florida College of Nursing and director of the McCrae Sleep Research Lab; interviewed, May 2026
  • Centers for Disease Control and Prevention: “Short Sleep Duration and Sleep Difficulties Among Adults: United States, 2024”
  • National Sleep Foundation: “2025 Sleep in America Poll”
  • Simon Fraser University: “Reprogramming Rest: Dr. Luc P. Beaudoin’s Science of Falling Asleep”
  • mySleepButton: “The Cognitive Shuffle Do-It-Yourself Instructions”
  • Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine: “Behavioral and psychological treatments for chronic insomnia disorder in adults: An American Academy of Sleep Medicine clinical practice guideline”

The post I Am a Terrible Sleeper, but This Clever Nighttime Trick Helped Me Fall Asleep in Minutes appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: In a Reading Slump? Here’s How to Finally Fall Back in Love with Books
articles.content:
我一直酷爱读书。小时候，我经常在卧室地板上或前院的树上飞快地翻阅悬疑系列小说。高中时，我常常熬夜阅读奇幻系列。现在，作为工作的一部分，我可以沉浸在新书发布中。但像每个人一样，有时我也会失去兴趣。有些月份，我甚至陷入全面的阅读低谷：几周内对拿起一本书几乎没有兴趣——或者在某些情况下，甚至难以集中注意力读完已经开始的书。

如果你最近对阅读感到无感，振作起来吧。你并不孤单，坦率地说，现代生活充满了干扰。但如果你想重新爱上阅读，这是可能的，无论过了多久。

为了启动一个能持续到冬天及以后的夏季阅读热潮，我向四位书迷寻求了他们摆脱阅读低谷的最佳建议。让我们开始吧。

加入免费的《读者文摘》书友会，获取好书、每月讨论、作者问答和书迷社区。

统计数据显示，阅读习惯正在全面下降。2025年的一项研究发现，每天为娱乐而阅读的美国人比例从2004年的28%下降到2023年的16%。过去20年，休闲阅读率每年下降约3%。2024年，只有48.5%的美国成年人表示在过去一年中至少读过一本书。

造成这种集体阅读低谷的原因可能有几个，其中一些源于日常科技使用的急剧增加。根据皮尤研究中心的数据，84%的美国成年人使用YouTube，71%的人在Facebook上。流媒体和滚动浏览占用了时间——可能是你原本会花在好书上的时间。一些专家还说，人工智能（AI）聊天机器人正在取代阅读书籍获取信息的需求。

首先，你需要想要开始更多地阅读。如果你正在寻找一个令人信服的理由来摆脱低谷，要知道阅读的好处难以夸大。它能缓解压力、改善睡眠、保护晚年认知能力。想要享受这些好处吗？请继续阅读，重新点燃你对书籍的热情。

就像重读喜爱的段落可以帮助你克服书后综合症一样，重读你最喜欢的一本书可以让你重新对阅读感到兴奋。

“选择一些经过验证的东西，”《浪漫英雄》的作者基尔斯蒂·格林伍德说。她解释说，重读你第一次无法放下的书可以让你确信自己会再次读完这本书。她补充说：“我发现这可以消除低谷带来的紧张恐惧，并提醒你为什么喜欢故事。”

《开放时代》的作者爱德华·施密特说，重读你年轻时喜欢的书也可以给你“一点怀旧的小刺激”。此外，他说，你可以用新的眼光享受这个故事。

真的！“每当我陷入阅读低谷时，我会拿起一本儿童读物——要么是重读一本经典老书，要么是一本我还没读过的当代中年级故事，”《读者文摘》书友会精选《这本书让我想起了你》的作者利比·佩奇说。“由于页数较少，专为挑剔的年轻读者设计的故事，如果你难以专注于任何太重的内容，儿童读物会很棒。”

如果一本优秀的儿童读物或青少年悬疑小说一时想不起来，佩奇建议选择安娜·詹姆斯的《页面与公司》奇幻系列或凯瑟琳·伦德尔的《不可能的生物》系列。

如果你把阅读当作待办事项清单上的一项任务，很容易陷入困境。美国图书馆协会2025-2026年主席山姆·赫尔米克说，拿起一本与你“应该”读的书不同的书是摆脱阅读低谷的好方法。

“比如图像小说、快节奏的科幻故事、散文……”赫尔米克说。“我发现动力比页数更重要；一旦我重新连接到发现的感觉，习惯往往会自动回归。”

谁说你必须用手拿着书才能阅读？美国人仍然更喜欢实体书而不是电子书和有声书，但根据皮尤研究中心的数据，有声书的收听量正在上升。而且有充分的理由：你可以在无法用眼睛阅读的时候听有声书。有声书可以通过让你在开车、骑车上班或在家做清洁或叠衣服等日常任务时听一本好小说来帮助你度过低谷。

“试着选择一本结合了你最喜欢爱好的书，希望这种激情能延续下去，”施密特说。

如果你喜欢去意大利旅行，为什么不拿起一本以罗马为背景的惊悚小说（比如《天使与恶魔》）或托斯卡纳的浪漫小说（想想《托斯卡纳艳阳下》）？或者如果你是一个狂热的棒球迷，你可以翻开新秀转职棒大联盟超级明星吉姆·莫里斯的励志传记，或前球员吉姆·布顿的内幕回忆录。

拿起一本薄薄的中篇小说可能比一本500页的史诗更能让你摆脱阅读低谷。这是目标设定的101条：如果你在处理任务时遇到困难，设定一个小而可实现的目标来逐步实现更大的目标。

阅读时，这看起来像把你的目标（摆脱阅读低谷）分解成一个小块（在一周内读完这本150页的短书）。这是一种技巧，但有效。如果你今年想读更多的书，短书会为你设定成功的机会。

这是你免费的DNF（在书界是“未完成”的意思）任何没有让你摆脱低谷的书的通行证。

“不要害怕放下你正在读的书，转而选择另一本更吸引你的书，”佩奇说。

此外，如果你想要一些不同的东西，没有理由不能稍后回到第一本书。有些书在特定季节最好——比如秋天的黑暗学术或与你下次旅行地点相匹配的氛围旅行书。

一些书籍有那种力量——通常是节奏和引人入胜的情节的神奇组合——能让你直接摆脱阅读低谷。以下是我个人最喜欢的一些书，还有美国图书馆协会主席赫尔米克推荐的几本：

获取《读者文摘》的《读起来》时事通讯，获取更多书籍、幽默、旅行、科技和有趣的事实。

关于专家

在《读者文摘》，我们分享了100多年的最爱书籍。我们与畅销书作者合作，包括苏珊·奥兰、珍妮特·埃文诺维奇和亚历克斯·黑利，其普利策奖获奖的《根》起源于并最初发表在杂志中的项目。通过《小说精选》（前身为《精选版》和《浓缩书》），《读者文摘》几十年来一直在出版小说选集。我们与一些最大的小说家合作，包括詹姆斯·帕特森、露丝·韦尔、克里斯汀·汉娜等。对于这篇文章，莱安德拉·比波特利用她作为长期记者的经验，确保所有信息准确，并为读者提供最佳建议。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠的来源支持它们，并随着时间的推移重新审视它们，以确保它们保持准确和最新。阅读更多关于我们的团队、我们的贡献者和我们的编辑政策。
articles.summary:
I’ve always devoured books. As a child, I flew through mystery series while sprawled out on my bedroom floor or perched in a tree in the front yard. In high school, I raced through fantasy series late into the night. Now I get to dive into new book releases as part of my job. But like everyone, sometimes I lose steam. Some months, I even sink into a full-fledged reading slump: weeks with little interest in picking up a book—or in some cases, trouble focusing long enough to finish the books I’ve already started.

If you’re feeling uninspired about reading these days, take heart. You’re not alone, and frankly, modern life is full of distractions. But if you want to fall back in love with reading, it is possible, no matter how long it’s been.

To kick-start a summer reading streak that lasts into winter and beyond, I turned to four book lovers for their best tips on how to get out of a reading slump. Let’s dive in.

Join the free Reader’s Digest Book Club for great reads, monthly discussions, author Q&As and a community of book lovers.

It’s not just you

Statistics show that reading habits are declining across the board. A 2025 study found that the number of Americans who read for pleasure each day has dropped from 28% in 2004 to 16% in 2023. Recreational reading rates have decreased about 3% per year for the last 20 years. In 2024, only 48.5% of American adults reported having read at least one book in the past year.

What’s causing this collective reading slump? Probably several factors, a few of which stem from a sharp rise in daily technology use. According to the Pew Research Center, 84% of American adults use YouTube, and 71% are on Facebook. Streaming and scrolling take time—possibly time you would’ve spent with a great book. Some experts also say that artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots are replacing the need to read books for information.

Get out of a reading slump

First things first: You need to want to start reading more. If you’re searching for a compelling reason to break out of your slump, know that it’s hard to overstate the benefits of reading. It does everything from relieve stress to improve sleep to protect against cognitive decline later in life. Want in on those perks? Read on for ways to reignite your passion for books.

1. Reread an old favorite

Just as rereading favorite passages can help you get over a book hangover, rereading one of your favorite books can help you feel excited about reading again.

“Pick something tried and true,” says Kirsty Greenwood, author of Romantic Hero. She explains that rereading something you couldn’t put down the first time can reassure you that you will be satisfied enough to make it through the book again. “I find this takes away the nervous dread that comes with a slump and reminds you why you love stories,” she adds.

Edward Schmit, author of the soon-to-be-released sports romance The Open Era, says rereading favorites from your younger years can also give you “a nice little kick of nostalgia.” Plus, he says, you can enjoy the story with new eyes.

2. Reach for a children’s book

Yes, really! “Whenever I’m in a reading slump, I reach for a children’s book—either a reread of an old classic or a contemporary middle-grade story that’s new to me,” says Libby Page, author of Reader’s Digest Book Club pick This Book Made Me Think of You. “With a lower page count and stories designed to grip picky young readers, children’s books can be great if you’re struggling to focus on anything too heavy.”

If a great children’s book or young adult page-turner doesn’t come to mind, Page suggests picking up anything by Anna James, author of the Pages & Co. fantasy series, or Katherine Rundell, author of the Impossible Creatures series.

3. Stop treating reading like homework

It’s easy to get in your head about reading if you treat it like an item to check off your to-do list. Sam Helmick, the 2025–2026 president of the American Library Association, says picking up a book that’s different from what you “should” be reading is a great way to get out of a reading slump.

“Like a graphic novel, a fast-moving sci-fi story, essays …” Helmick says. “I’ve found that momentum matters more than page count; once I reconnect with the feeling of discovery, the habit tends to return on its own.”

4. Download an audiobook

Who says you need to hold a book in your hands to read it? Americans still prefer physical books to e-books and audiobooks, but audiobook listening is on the rise, according to the Pew Research Center. And for good reason: You can listen to an audiobook at times when it wouldn’t be possible to read with your eyes. Audiobooks can help you push through a slump by letting you listen to a great novel while driving, biking to work or doing mundane tasks like cleaning or folding laundry at home.

5. Look for a book in a world you love

“Try choosing a book that incorporates one of your favorite hobbies, and hopefully that passion carries over,” Schmidt says.

If you love traveling to Italy, why not pick up a thriller set in Rome (like Angels & Demons) or a romance in Tuscany (think: Under the Tuscan Sun)? Or if you’re an avid baseball fan, you could crack open the inspiring biography of rookie-turned-MLB-superstar Jim Morris or the insider memoir of former player Jim Bouton.

6. Try a shorter book

Picking up a slim novella may be more likely to kick you out of a reading slump than a 500-page epic. It’s goal-setting 101: If you’re struggling with a task, set a small, attainable goal to chip away at the larger aim.

With reading, that looks like breaking down your goal (getting out of a reading slump) into a small chunk (finish this 150-page short book by the end of the week). It’s a bit of a hack, but it works. If you want to read more books this year, shorter books set you up for success.

7. Give yourself permission to “DNF”

Here’s your free pass to DNF (that’s “did not finish” in the book world) any book that’s not kicking you out of your slump

“Don’t be afraid to put down the book you’re reading and turn to something else that’s calling more loudly to you,” Page says.

Plus, there’s no reason you can’t return to the first book later if you’re craving something different. Some books are best in specific seasons—like dark academia in the fall or atmospheric travel books matched to where you’re traveling next.

Read your way out of a book slump

Some books have that oomph—often a magical mix of pace and gripping plot—to kick you right out of a reading slump. Here are some of my personal favorites, plus a few recommended by book-loving ALA president Helmick:

  • Godfall by Van Jensen. “This book moves with real energy and imagination while still asking big questions about power, identity and humanity. It’s immersive without feeling dense,” Helmick says.
  • Warning Signs by Tracy Sierra. This survivalist thriller (a Reader’s Digest Book Club pick!) about a father-son ski trip pulled me in and had me rooting for young Zach right away. But unlike many edge-of-your-seat thrillers, this one also made my heart ache in a lasting way—yes, tears were shed.
  • Fahrenheit 451 by Ray Bradbury. “It’s fast, vivid, unsettling and deeply relevant in an age of distraction and information overload,” Helmick says. “Even readers who haven’t picked up a book in months often find themselves pulled quickly into its urgency and atmosphere.”
  • The Night Hunter by Natalie Moss. I love that the action starts right away in this energetic novel about a woman who heads into the South African bush after receiving a cryptic note from her dead mother. You’ll keep reading to find out which is scarier: being hunted by a leopard, a buffalo or a human enemy?
  • Inside the Box by David Epstein. Helmick loves recommending this nonfiction title to people feeling creatively or intellectually stuck. “It’s accessible, energizing and often helps readers reconnect with the joy of thinking and learning in a new way.”
  • Me Talk Pretty One Day by David Sedaris. Reading a collection of Sedaris’s irreverent essays and short stories doesn’t feel like reading a whole book. You’ll climb out of your slump one short episode at a time, smiling wryly all the while.
  • The Voyage of the Dawn Treader by C.S. Lewis. I still haven’t seen the movie because why ruin perfection? Rereading any of the Narnia series feels a little like remembering my first crush: cue the dramatic middle-school feels about friendship and love and adventure as if it’s the very first time.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more books, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

About the experts

  • Sam Helmick (they/them) is the 2025–2026 president of the American Library Association and past president of the Iowa Library Association. They hold a master’s in library and information science from the University of Illinois.
  • Kirsty Greenwood is the London-based author of laugh-out-loud romantic comedies, including The Love of My Afterlife and Romantic Hero.
  • Edward Schmit is a New York City–based author whose debut novel, The Open Era, combines three of his favorite things: queer love stories, mental health awareness and tennis.
  • Libby Page is the U.K.-based author of several heartwarming novels, including This Book Made Me Think of You and The Lifeline.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’ve been sharing our favorite books for over 100 years. We’ve worked with bestselling authors including Susan Orlean, Janet Evanovich and Alex Haley, whose Pulitzer Prize–winning Roots grew out of a project funded by and originally published in the magazine. Through Fiction Favorites (formerly Select Editions and Condensed Books), Reader’s Digest has been publishing anthologies of abridged novels for decades. We’ve worked with some of the biggest names in fiction, including James Patterson, Ruth Ware, Kristin Hannah and more. The Reader’s Digest Book Club, helmed by Books Editor Tracey Neithercott, introduces readers to even more of today’s best fiction by upcoming, bestselling and award-winning authors. For this piece, Leandra Beabout tapped her experience as a longtime journalist who covers books for Reader’s Digest to ensure that all information is accurate and offers the best possible advice to readers. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Sam Helmick, president of the American Library Association; email interview, May 15, 2026
  • Kirsty Greenwood, author of The Love of My Afterlife and Romantic Hero; email interview, May 2026
  • Edward Schmit, author of The Open Era; email interview, May 2026
  • Libby Page, author of This Book Made Me Think of You and The Lifeline; email interview, May 2026
  • iScience, A Cell Press Journal: “The decline in reading for pleasure over 20 years of the American Time Use Survey”
  • The National Endowment for the Arts: “Federal Data on Reading for Pleasure: All Signs Show a Slump”
  • Pew Research Center: “Americans’ Social Media Use 2025”
  • The Conversation: “AI is making reading books feel obsolete – and students have a lot to lose”
  • Pew Research Center: “Americans still opt for print books over digital or audio versions; few are in book clubs”

Bookworms Reveal How to Read More Books

Expert-Backed Tips to Read Faster

How to Clean Your Books (Without Ruining Them)

The post In a Reading Slump? Here’s How to Finally Fall Back in Love with Books appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Landman’s  Sam Elliot Shares a Sobering Update on His Physical Health: “I’m Paying for It Now”
articles.content:
几十年来，山姆·艾略特一直以扮演粗犷的牛仔、牧场主和你绝对不想招惹的硬汉形象而闻名。如今，这位81岁的演员表示，他最新角色所面临的某些身体上的挑战，似乎比他以往所扮演的角色更真实。在接受《综艺》杂志5月26日（周二）分享的一次采访中，艾略特谈到了在《地界》（Landman）中饰演T.L.诺里斯这一角色时，一些情节让他感同身受。

“事实上，那一幕确实非常贴近我目前的身心状态，”艾略特坦言。他提到的那场戏，是第二季中一个特别触动人心的情节。“从身体上来说，这正是我现在所处的状况。不是心理上，而是身体上。我的一生都很艰难，我一直是运动员，也一直是个劳工，现在我正在为此付出代价。”

他提到的那个场景，是剧中一个最令人心碎的时刻之一：T.L.意识到自己无法再靠自己的力量爬出游泳池后，他被困在了那里。当他的儿子汤米·诺里斯（由比利·鲍勃·松顿饰演）赶来帮助他时，T.L.含泪说道：“我的头脑依然清晰，这简直是一种诅咒。我只能坐在这里，清楚地感受到我的身体正在以各种方式背叛我。”

在过去的六十年里，艾略特凭借在《谋杀绿脚强》、《公路休息室》和《一个明星的诞生》等影片中的出色表现，逐渐成为业内最知名的演员之一，这些作品让他在2019年首次获得奥斯卡提名。虽然他承认随着年龄增长，他能扮演的角色类型发生了变化，但如今他听起来更多是反思，而不是抱怨。

“我不会出去再扮演年轻英俊的男主角了，”艾略特告诉媒体。“但事实是，能有一个我能够投入并真正享受的角色，这是一件美妙的事情。”

这种观点是他在职业生涯早期就培养起来的。他有意尝试构建一个有长远眼光的事业，而不是追逐每一个出现的片约。

“我从一开始就决定，如果我想要有一个长久的职业生涯，我就不能只为了钱而工作，不能什么片约都接，”他解释道。“我必须有选择性和判断力地参与项目。”

尽管他坦承了老化带来的身体负担，但听起来艾略特仍然像往常一样全身心投入工作。

据《地界》的同事所说，这位资深演员几乎不可能被请出片场。2025年12月，他的同事凯拉·华莱士告诉《人物》杂志，艾略特在拍摄期间几乎从不离开片场，即使在没有安排他拍摄的场景时也是如此。

“他总是会找一个地方坐着——长凳、苹果箱，什么都行。他并不挑剔，”华莱士解释道。“他只是很乐意坐在那里，成为我们拍摄的一部分，看着我们表演。”

这种敬业精神在如今已经很难得，但这也解释了为什么艾略特能在60多年里一直保持着如此受人尊敬的银屏形象。他只是想继续做他热爱的事业。

在谷歌上添加读者文摘！
这则新闻最初发表于读者文摘。
articles.summary:
For decades, Sam Elliot built a career playing rugged cowboys, ranchers and tough guys you definitely don’t want to cross in the wild. These days, though, the 81-year-old actor says some of the physical struggles facing his latest character feel a little less fictional.

In an interview with Variety shared on Tuesday, May 26, Elliot opened up about portraying T.L. Norris on Landman and admitted that one especially emotional storyline from Season 2 hit a little too close to home.

“The truth of it is, [that scene] is very true to where I am at this point in my life physically,” Elliot shared. “Not mentally, by physically. I’ve had a hard life. I’ve been an athlete all my life, and I’ve been a laborer all my life, and I’m paying for it now.”

The moment he referenced came during one of the show’s most heartbreaking scenes, when T.L. becomes trapped in a swimming pool after realizing he can no longer pull himself out on his own. As his son Tommy Norris (played by Billy Bob Thorton) rushed to help him, T.L. tearfully admitted, “It’s a curse my mind is sharp. I get to sit here and process all the ways my body is failing me.”

Over the last six decades, Elliot has quietly become one of the most recognizable actors in the business thanks to his unforgettable roles in The Big Lebowski, Roadhouse and A Star Is Born, which earned him his first Academy Award nomination in 2019. And while he admits aging has changed the types of roles he can play, he sounds more reflective than bitter about it these days.

“I’m not going to go out and play the young leading, dashing man at this stage,” Elliot told the outlet. “The fact that there’s a part that I can get into and really enjoy doing, it’s a wonderful thing.”

That perspective is something he developed early in his career. Rather than chasing every paycheck that came along, Elliot said he intentionally tried to build a career with longevity in mind.

“I determined early on that if I wanted to have a long career, I couldn’t work for the dollar and do anything that came along,” he explained. “I had to be selective and judicious about what it was that I got involved with.”

Despite opening up about the physical toll aging has taken, it sounds like Elliot still approaches work with the same dedication he’s always had.

According to his Landman co-star, the veteran actor is practically impossible to get off set. Actress Kayla Wallace told People in December 2025 that Elliot “never leaves” during filming, even when he’s not scheduled to shoot scenes himself.

“He’ll find a place to sit—a bench, an apple box, whatever. He’s not fussy,” Wallace explained. “He’s just happy to sit there and be a part of it and watch all of us perform.”

That kind of dedication is hard to find nowadays, but it may also explain why Elliot has managed to remain such a respected presence on screen for more than 60 years. He just wants to keep doing the work he loves.

Add Reader’s Digest on Google!

Sources:

  • Variety: “Landman Star Sam Elliot Is Still Pursuing Honesty in His Art”
  • People: “Sam Elliot ‘Never Leaves’ the Landman Set, Even When He’s Done Working”

The post <i>Landman’s </i> Sam Elliot Shares a Sobering Update on His Physical Health: “I’m Paying for It Now” appeared first on Reader's Digest.
